title: Seismic Hazard Mapping In Japan: effects on land and real estate prices
text:
Motivation
What should we care?
Taking a step back, this work addresses the following questions: are people willing to leave in more risk exposed areas? Are they even aware where are the more likely threats, despite the sensitization policies? Are people’s responses different when such a natural disaster occurs?
Japan is likely to experience a big earthquake in the next 30 years, namely the Nankai Through. However, seismic risk more broadly threatens other places, such as The Big One megatrust eathquake, which is anticipated along Western North America.
Finally, the same logic may apply to climate change. There might be a similar logic for other types of natural disasters, such as floods, droughts, typhoons, etc. If that is the case, then investors, and even more interestingly the policy makers, might want to know where to invest in the near futur taking into account the risk factor.

Data wrangling

The MLIT Dataset
The data used in this analysis comes from the MLIT Dataset, which contains records of real estate price transactions across Japan since 2003.
You can select the prefectures you want to download. I chose to download all of them and ended up with 47 datasets, corresponding to the 47 prefectures of Japan.


Wrangling the data
I use Stata to combine the 47 datasets and save the big one file as data_mlit, since Stata is more convenient for handling encoding issues. Note that it might take some time.

cd "/Users/anthony/Desktop/master_thesis/data/raw/mlit"

local prefectures : dir "." files "*.csv"
clear

foreach prefecture of local prefectures {
    preserve
    import delimited "`prefecture'", clear
    tempfile temp
    save `temp'
    restore 
    append using `temp'
}

save data_mlit, replace



Importing the data

title: Seismic Hazard Mapping In Japan: effects on land and real estate prices
text:
What should we care?
Taking a step back, this work addresses the following questions: what is the risk premium people have to pay to live in safer places? Are people willing to stay in areas under the threat of natural disasters? Are housing spatial inequalities being reshaped by risk mapping to some extent? Are they even aware of such threats, despite the sensitization policies?
As for Japan, this is a particularly burning issue. The country frequently experience earthquakes, and it is likely to be struck by what is called a mega-earthquake in the next 30 years (Nankai Through), experts say. On a global scale, The so-called “The big One” mega-earthquake is believed to strike Asia as well as along Western North America. The important thing with earthquakes is that one cannot tell when they occur, so that it threatens everywhere every time.
The same logic may apply to climate change. As other types of natural disasters are increasing (such as floods, droughts and typhoons among others), investors as well as policy makers may be interested in the real cost of investing in this type of areas.

JMA records include all seismic activity observations. They are to be converted to csv first, ensuring that they are well encoded.
Records include of course latitude and longitude in DMS format. Then the following code convert those variables in the CRS WGS84 (epsg:4326) format:

# Convert latitude_deg / lat and longitude_deg / lon in DMS to WGS84 (epsg:4326)
def dms_to_decimals(latlon_deg: int = 31, latlon: int = 1176):
    minute = latlon // 100
    second = latlon % 100
    return latlon_deg + minute / 60 + second / 3600
  
jma_damage["latitude"] = jma_damage.apply(
    lambda row : dms_to_decimals(row["latitude_deg"], row["lat"]),
    axis = 1)

jma_damage["longitude"] = jma_damage.apply(
    lambda row : dms_to_decimals(row["longitude_deg"], row["lon"]),
    axis = 1)

title: Theory and Empirical Design
text:
Let \(p\) denote the subjective probability that a property will be hit by an earthquake. \(p\) depends on both the information set \(i\) that individuals possess about seismic risks (such as recent events, media coverage, etc.), and \(r\), the objective attributes that make earthquakes more likely to occur, such as being located on seismic faults. Note that \(\pi\), the objective probability of an earthquake, is included in \(i\) in our case, since maps are provided and publicly available.
The Hedonic Price Function is given by: \[
P = P(Z, r, p(i, r))
\]
where \(P\) denotes the price of the house and \(Z\) denotes an additional set of structural, environmental, and locational characteristics not related to the seismic hazard maps.
Following Brookshire et al. (1985), the decision of agents is modeled using a state-dependent expected utility function: \[
EU = p(i, r) \cdot U^E[Z, r, Q] + (1 - p(i, r)) \cdot U^{NE}[Z, r, Q],
\] where \(U^E(\cdot)\) is the homeowner’s utility in the event of an earthquake, and \(U^{NE}(\cdot)\) is the homeowner’s utility when no such event occurs. Here, \(Q\) is a composite commodity, and the budget constraint of the homeowner is: \[
M = P(Z, r, p(i, r)) + Q.
\]
Maximizing expected utility with respect to \(p\), subject to the budget constraint, and then dividing by the expected marginal utility of income yields: \[
\frac{\partial P}{\partial p} = \frac{U^{E} - U^{NE}}{p(i,r)\frac{\partial U^{E}}{\partial Q} + \left(1 - p(i,r)\right)\frac{\partial U^{NE}}{\partial Q}}.
\]

title: Design
text:
Most of the papers set risk as a property attribute, and then use hedonic price model introduced by Rosen (1974). I follow this approach to begin, with the following equation:
\[y_{it}^{(k)} = \alpha_0^{(k)} + \alpha_c(i) + \gamma_q(t)+ {x_{it}^{(k)}}' \beta_1+ {x_{\cdot t}^{(k)}}' \beta_2+ \bar{x}_{it}^{(k)}{}' \beta_3+ r_{it}(\psi)' \beta_4+ \bar{u}^{(k)},\]

with \(y_{it}^{(k)}\) the housing prices.

title: Theory and Empirical Design
text:
First approach: Hedonic price model
Most of the papers set risk as a property attribute, and the use hedonic price approach as introduced by Rosen (1974). This approach uses DiD terminology:
\[\begin{equation}
\ln P_{it} = \alpha_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{J} \alpha_j Z_{ij} + \beta r_i + \gamma Seismic_i + \delta Post_{it} + \theta_t (Post_{it} \times Seismic_i) + \varepsilon_{it}
\end{equation}\]
- \(\ln P_{it}\): log property price for unit \(i\) at time \(t\)
- \(Z_{ij}\): vector of property and location controls
- \(r_i\): location fixed effects (prefectures)
- \(Seismic_i\): baseline seismic risk exposure (over \(p = 0.3\))
- \(Earthquake_{it}\): earthquake occurrence/intensity at time \(t\)
- \(Earthquake_{it} \times Seismic_i\): interaction capturing heterogeneous effects
In practice, this is my first attempt:
regress log_price ///
    sqft age ///
    parking_y ///
    walk_distance1 super_distance hospital_distance park_distance ///
    pop_total_linear ///
    employement_linear_rate ///
    net_migration ///
    foreigners_linear_share ///
    c.earthquake##c.seismic ///
    i.prefecture, robust

title: Design
text:
Setup
My economic outcomes are sales and rentals. Secondary outcomes may include earthquake insurance subscription and municipality expenses for earthquake prevention. The variable of interest is any earthquake over magnitude 6 occurring in Japan. One unit of observation is one property to be sell or to be rent. Those are available throughout the country, providing a great source of variation.
To put it clearly:

Treatment: earthquake over magnitude 6 ocuring in the whole country
Main Outcomes: Sales and rentals
Units: properties to be sell or rented
Source of variation:

all over Japan
for many types of buildings
with different structures

Comparison group: exposed and not exposed areas.

Difference-in-Differences Methodology

Treated group: \(p > 0.26\)
Control group: \(p < 0.06\)
I discard the observations with intermediate values
Let’s denote \(\text{Exposed}_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } p_i > 0.26 \\ 0 & \text{if } p_i < 0.06 \end{cases}\)

\(r_i\): prefecture and year fixed effects
\(Exposed_i\): baseline seismic risk exposure
\(Post_{it}\): Announce \(i\) is published \(x\) days after a “big earthquake”. Right now \(x = 30\).
\(Post_{it} \times Exposed_i\): interaction capturing treatment effects

title: Workflow
text:
CLEANING

Let’s clean the Liffull Homes datasets
The Liffull Homes data consists of actually two types of datasets. The first one, that I will call “monthly”, includes all the listings from July 2015 to June 2017. The second one, “period”, includes all the listings from October 2018 to December 2014. Unfortunately, those two do not match perfectly well.
Let’s begin with the “monthly”. This one is provided as 24 .tsv files, for each year from July 2015 to June 2017. First, let’s append them. Since they are huge files (around 70GB), I proceed using Python and the Duckdb library:

# Get the new column names from the codebook
names_codebook = pd.read_excel("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/data_spec_monthly_20181113.xlsx", 
                               sheet_name=1, 
                               skiprows=2)
names_new = names_codebook["フィールド名"].tolist() # フィールド名 is the list of variable names in English

# Append
duckdb.sql(f"""
    COPY (
        SELECT * FROM read_csv_auto(
            '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/*.tsv',
            sep='\t',
            union_by_name=true,
            names={names_new}
        )
    ) TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents1_combined.parquet'
    (FORMAT PARQUET, COMPRESSION 'zstd')
""")

Many of the columns which can be discarded and I create a lighter parquet file:




con.execute("""
COPY (
    SELECT
        bukken_id::VARCHAR AS id,
        start_date::DATE AS start_date,
        end_date::DATE AS end_date,
        bukken_type::BIGINT AS type,
        addr1_1::BIGINT AS prefecture,
        addr1_2::BIGINT AS city_district,
        eki1::BIGINT AS eki1,
        bus_time1::BIGINT AS bus1_time,
        walk_distance1::BIGINT AS station1_distance,
        bus_time2::BIGINT AS bus2_time,
        walk_distance2::BIGINT AS station2_distance,
        land_youto::BIGINT AS land_use_zone,
        land_toshi::BIGINT AS urban_plannning,
        land_chisei::BIGINT AS topography,
        land_area::DOUBLE AS land_area,
        land_shidou::DOUBLE AS private_road_area,
        land_setback_flg::BIGINT AS setback_y,
        land_kenpei::BIGINT AS building_coverage_ratio,
        land_youseki::BIGINT AS floor_area_ratio,
        land_right::BIGINT as land_right,
        land_kokudohou::BIGINT AS land_use_planning,
        house_kouzou::BIGINT AS building_structure,
        house_area::DOUBLE AS sqft,
        house_kaisuu::BIGINT AS building_nb_of_floors,
        house_kaisuu_chika::BIGINT,
        kenchiku_date::BIGINT AS construction_date,
        flg_new::BIGINT AS new_y,
        room_kaisuu::BIGINT AS floor,
        balcony_area::DOUBLE AS balcony_area,
        madori_number_all::BIGINT AS rooms,
        madori_kind_all::BIGINT AS floor_plan_type,
        money_room::BIGINT AS price1,
        money_tsubo::BIGINT AS price_tsubo,
        money_kyoueki::BIGINT AS common_area_fee,
        money_combo::BIGINT AS price,
        parking_kubun::BIGINT AS parking_y,
        school_ele_distance::BIGINT AS school_ele_distance,
        school_jun_distance::BIGINT AS school_jun_distance,
        convenience_distance::BIGINT AS convenience_distance,
        super_distance::BIGINT AS super_distance,
        hospital_distance::BIGINT AS hospital_distance,
        nl::DOUBLE AS lat,
        el::DOUBLE AS lon
    FROM read_parquet(
        '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_combined.parquet'
    )
)
TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_renamed.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET);
"""
)

Now, to get a event lighter dataset, I drop where money_roomand house_aear/ land_area are missing. Taking a step forward to the sample selection, I keep only land for sales, condominium or apartments.


See property type codebook

con.execute("""
COPY (
    SELECT *
    FROM read_parquet(
        '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_renamed.parquet'
    )
    WHERE
        price IS NOT NULL AND price != 0
    AND (
        (sqft IS NOT NULL AND sqft != 0)
        OR
        (land_area IS NOT NULL AND land_area != 0)
    )
        AND type IN (1101, 1201, 1202, 1203, 1204, 3101, 3102, 3103, 3104, 3105, 3212)
)
TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_filtered.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET);
""")

It is now possible to merge this dataset with the J-SHIS hazard maps.Here they are:

shapefile_map = {
    2014: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2015: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2014-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2016: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2016-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2016-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2017: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2017-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2017-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2018: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2018-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2018-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2019: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2019-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2019-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2020: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2020-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2020-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2021: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2021-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2021-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2022: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2023: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp',
    2024: '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/maps/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE/P-Y2022-MAP-AVR-TTL_MTTL-SHAPE.shp'
}

You might notice that for years 2015, 2023 and 2024, the associated shapefiles covers some other years. The reason is that they do not exist. I decide to take the previous risk map for 2014 and the 2022 for 2013 and 2024, acknowledging that this is a lesser evil in my research design, but arguing that this should not be harmful since those maps, even though evolving year by year, are computed using a Poisson process over decades and do not move that much from one year to another.
The important thing to pay attention to is to watch out for CRS issue. The coodebook says that the data are EPSG:4326 encoded. However, the maps are EPSG:4612. Those two differ only by a few meters but still let’s convert during merging.
To merge, I use once again SQL:

con.execute("INSTALL spatial;")
con.execute("LOAD spatial;")

To optimize performance, I load the data and proceed with subset for each year:

# =========================================================
# 1. LOAD RENTS1
# =========================================================
con.execute("""
CREATE TABLE rents1 AS
SELECT
    *,
    YEAR(start_date) AS year
FROM read_parquet(
    '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents1_filtered.parquet'
)
""")

# =========================================================
# 2. LOOP OVER YEARS
# =========================================================
years = range(2014, 2018)

for year in years:

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE rents1_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM rents1
        WHERE year = {year}
          AND lat IS NOT NULL
          AND lon IS NOT NULL
    """)

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE map_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM ST_Read('{shapefile_map[year]}')
    """)

    # SPATIAL MERGE
    con.execute(f"""
    CREATE TABLE rents1_{year}_geo AS
    SELECT
        r.*,
        s.CODE        AS mesh_code,
        s.T30_I45_PS,
        s.T30_I50_PS,
        s.T30_I55_PS,
        s.T30_I60_PS
    FROM rents1_{year} r
    LEFT JOIN map_{year} s
        ON ST_Within(
            ST_Transform(ST_Point(r.lon, r.lat), 'EPSG:4326', 'EPSG:4612'),
            s.geom
        )
""")

    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE rents1_{year}")
    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE map_{year}")

All the rents1_\(*\) remain to be appended:

con.execute("""
CREATE TABLE rents_geo AS
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2014_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2015_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2016_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents1_2017_geo
""")

Finally, let’s save this in a rents1.parquetfile.

con.execute("""
COPY rents_geo TO
    '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/processed/rents1.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET)
""")

Now I have processed with “monthly”, let’s clean the “period” data. Those ones cover the listings from October 2018 to November 2024. Two datasets are attached: the first one is called “bukken_rent_v1.0.1” and deals with rents, and the second one, “bukken_sale_v1.0.1”, is about the sales.
Two main differences: the names and the CRS. Let’s easily rename the rents2 dataset as the rents1 dataset while selecting the associated columns.

con.execute("""
COPY (
    SELECT
        id::VARCHAR AS id,
        pub_start_date::DATE AS start_date,
        pub_end_date::DATE AS end_date,
        bukken_type::BIGINT AS type,
        addr1_1::BIGINT AS prefecture,
        addr1_2::BIGINT AS city_district,
        eki1::BIGINT AS eki1,
        bus_time1::BIGINT AS bus1_time,
        walk_distance1::BIGINT AS station1_distance,
        bus_time2::BIGINT AS bus2_time,
        walk_distance2::BIGINT AS station2_distance,
        land_youto::BIGINT AS land_use_zone,
        land_toshi::BIGINT AS urban_plannning,
        land_chisei::BIGINT AS topography,
        land_area::DOUBLE AS land_area,
        land_shidou::DOUBLE AS private_road_area,
        land_setback_flg::BIGINT AS setback_y,
        land_kenpei::BIGINT AS building_coverage_ratio,
        land_youseki::BIGINT AS floor_area_ratio,
        land_right::BIGINT AS land_right,
        land_kokudohou::BIGINT AS land_use_planning,
        house_kouzou::BIGINT AS building_structure,
        house_area::DOUBLE AS sqft,
        house_kaisuu::BIGINT AS building_nb_of_floors,
        house_kaisuu_chika::BIGINT AS house_kaisuu_chika,
        kenchiku_date::BIGINT AS construction_date,
        flg_new::BIGINT AS new_y,
        room_kaisuu::BIGINT AS floor,
        balcony_area::DOUBLE AS balcony_area,
        madori_number_all::BIGINT AS rooms,
        madori_kind_all::BIGINT AS floor_plan_type,
        money_room::BIGINT AS price1,
        money_tsubo::BIGINT AS price_tsubo,
        money_kyoueki::BIGINT AS common_area_fee,
        money_combo::BIGINT AS price,
        parking_kubun::BIGINT AS parking_y,
        school_ele_distance::BIGINT AS school_ele_distance,
        school_jun_distance::BIGINT AS school_jun_distance,
        convenience_distance::BIGINT AS convenience_distance,
        super_distance::BIGINT AS super_distance,
        hospital_distance::BIGINT AS hospital_distance,
        nl::DOUBLE AS lat,
        el::DOUBLE AS lon
    FROM read_csv_auto('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents_2024/bukken_rent_v1.0.1.tsv', delim='\t', ignore_errors=true)
)
TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/pre-processed/rents2_renamed.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET);
""")

I apply the same sample selection as rents1
Let’s tackle the second issue: the CRS. This has been challenging but finally break down. lon and lat are encoded differently than in rents1. After some research, lat and lon are in

con.execute("""
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE rents2 AS
SELECT
    * EXCLUDE (lat, lon),
    YEAR(start_date) AS year,
    lat / 3600000.0 AS lat,
    lon / 3600000.0 AS lon
FROM '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents2_filtered.parquet';
""")

Using the same loop as before, I merge rents2 with the years following:

# =========================================================
#  LOOP OVER YEARS
# =========================================================
years = range(2018, 2024)

for year in years:

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE rents2_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM rents2
        WHERE year = {year}
          AND lat IS NOT NULL
          AND lon IS NOT NULL
    """)

    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE map_{year} AS
        SELECT *
        FROM ST_Read({shapefile_map})
    """)

    # SPATIAL MERGE
    con.execute(f"""
        CREATE TABLE rents2_{year}_geo AS
        SELECT
            r.*,
            ST_Transform(
                ST_Point(r.lon, r.lat),
                'EPSG:4326',
                'EPSG:4612'
            ) AS point_geom,
            s.CODE        AS mesh_code,
            s.T30_I45_PS,
            s.T30_I50_PS,
            s.T30_I55_PS,
            s.T30_I60_PS
        FROM rents2_{year} r
        LEFT JOIN map_{year} s
            ON ST_Within(
                ST_Transform(ST_Point(r.lon, r.lat), 'EPSG:4326', 'EPSG:4612'),
                s.geom
            )
    """)

    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE rents2_{year}")
    con.execute(f"DROP TABLE map_{year}")
    
# =========================================================
#  APPEND
# =========================================================

con.execute("""
CREATE TABLE rents_geo AS
    SELECT * FROM rents_2018_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2019_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2020_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2021_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2022_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2023_geo
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM rents_2024_geo
""")

con.execute("""
COPY rents_geo TO
    '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents2.parquet'
(FORMAT PARQUET)
""")

With a few name exceptions, the last dataset (“bukken_sale_v1.0.1”) follows the same logic and I end up with three datasets that I finally append.

# =========================================================
#  APPEND THE THREE LIFULL HOMES DATASETS
# =========================================================

con.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE data_all AS
    SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents1.parquet')
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/rents2.parquet')
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/liffull_homes/sales.parquet')
""")

# This is intermediate step to discard potential duplicates
con.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE clean_data AS
    SELECT DISTINCT * FROM data_all
""")

con.execute("""
    COPY clean_data
    TO '/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/liffull.parquet'
    (FORMAT PARQUET)
""")

con.execute("DROP TABLE raw_data")
con.execute("DROP TABLE clean_data")



Let’s clean the earthquakes records from the JMA
Now, let’s turn to the JMA files. They can be download for each year and contain every single earthquake records. I download them for years from 2015 to 2024 and I use first Python and second Stata to clean and select them.

import pandas as pd
import glob

First, I append them.

files = glob.glob("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/h*")

# First, I convert the files in txt format
def convert_file_to_txt(file_input, file_output):
    with open(file_input, "r", encoding = "utf-8") as f:
        lines = f.readlines()

    with open (file_output, "w", encoding = "utf-8") as f:
        for line in lines:
            f.write(line)
            
for i, file in enumerate(files):
    year = 2015 + i
    convert_file_to_txt(file, f"/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_{year}.txt")
 
# Now, I append   
files_txt = glob.glob("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_*.txt")
files_txt.sort()

with open("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for file in files_txt:
        with open(file, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f1:
            f.write(f1.read())

Second, I use the documentation provided by the JMA (https://www.data.jma.go.jp/eqev/data/bulletin/data/shindo/format_e.txt) to parse the lines.

def parse_line(line):
    try:
        return {
            "date":             f"{line[1:5]}-{line[5:7]}-{line[7:9]}",
            "magnitude":        line[52:54].strip(),
            "max_intensity":    line[61].strip(),
            # The CRS is once again different. The following formula converts from DMS to WGS84 (epsg:4326) format.
            "eq_lat":           int(line[21:24]) + (int(line[24:28]) / 100) / 60,
            "eq_lon":           int(line[32:36]) + (int(line[36:40]) / 100) / 60,
            "damage_class":     line[62].strip(),
            "tsunami_class":    line[63].strip(),
            "district_number":  line[64].strip(),
            "region_number":    line[65:68].strip(),
        }
    except:
        return None

with open("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

data = [parsed for line in lines if (parsed := parse_line(line)) is not None]

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df.to_stata("/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.dta", write_index=False)

Switching in Stata, I proceed to the sample selection when it comes to the earthquakes.

use "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/jma/jma_combined.dta", clear

* DROP
drop if inlist(magnitude, "A0", "A1", "A2", "A3", "A4")
destring magnitude, replace
drop if magnitude == .
drop if magnitude < 50

* KEEP only the seisms with max_intensity >= 5
keep if inlist(max_intensity, "7", "A", "B", "C", "D")
replace max_intensity = "5"   if max_intensity == "A"
replace max_intensity = "5.5" if max_intensity == "B"
replace max_intensity = "6"   if max_intensity == "C"
replace max_intensity = "6.5" if max_intensity == "D"
destring max_intensity, replace

* Standardize magnitude
replace magnitude = magnitude / 10

* Destring other variables
destring damage_class tsunami_class, replace
replace damage_class  = 0 if damage_class  == .
replace tsunami_class = 0 if tsunami_class == .
destring district_number, replace

* For the seisms occuring the same day, keep only the strongest one
bysort date: keep if _n == _N

* Create date
gen eq_date = date(date, "YMD")
drop date

* Preparing an index for the future merge
gen eq_index = _n

* Get it in the right order and save
order eq_date eq_index magnitude max_intensity eq_lat eq_lon damage_class tsunami_class district_number region_number
save "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/clean/eq_records.dta", replace



Let’s clean data from other control variables
To capture the attractiveness of a property, I collect other data from e-stat and some other macroeconomic variables.

********************************************************************************
* MERGE RAW FILES

local files pop adm lab edu dwe med cul
local pop_opts `" clear "'
local other_opts `" rowrange(9) varnames(9) clear "'

* Load population first
import delimited using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/e-stat/pop.csv", clear
drop if year < 2015
tempfile estat_combined
save `estat_combined', replace emptyok

* Merge with the rest of the datasets
foreach f in adm lab edu dwe med cul {
    import delimited using "/Users/anthony/Documents/master_thesis/data/raw/e-stat/`f'.csv", ///
        rowrange(9) varnames(9) clear
    drop items
    drop if year < 2015
    merge 1:1 year areacode using `estat_combined', nogenerate
    save `estat_combined', replace
}

* Convert to numeric
use `estat_combined', clear
destring g1201_numberofcommunitycentersnu - a7101_numberofhouseholdstotalhou, ///
    replace ignore("***" "-" ",")

Many values are missing. For example, the variables from the “population” category come from the Japanese census which happens every five years. I proceed three ways to fill in missing variables:

Linear interpolation

for data available only for years 2015 and 2020, mainly the ones from the census, I compute the linear line coefficients between those two variables and complete the missing variables depending on the year.



********************************************************************************
* FILLING IN MISSING VALUES

* Linear interpolation
* Those for which 2015 and 2020 are available
foreach var of varlist pop_total median_age lifeexp_male lifeexp_female pop_u15 pop_o65 did_pop foreigners households pop_total2 employed unemployed labor_force skilled_workers selfemp_with selfemp_without commute_in commute_out {

    * Create base
    gen `var'_base = .
    bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_base = `var'[1]

    * Linear interpolation coefficients
    gen `var'_delta = .
    bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_delta = (`var'[6] / `var'[1]) ^ (1/5)

    * Interpolation computation
    gen `var'_linear = .
    forvalues y = 2015/2023 {
        local t = `y' - 2015
        replace `var'_linear = `var'_base * (`var'_delta ^ `t') if year == `y'
    }

    * Drop temporary var
    drop `var'_delta
    drop `var'_base
}


Regression

this method is based on the previous one, but more than two years are available.



* Regression
gen inmigrants_reg = inmigrants
gen outmigrants_reg = outmigrants
foreach var in inmigrants outmigrants {

    gen yhat = .

    levelsof areacode, local(areas)

    foreach a of local areas {
        count if areacode == `a' & !missing(year, `var')
        
        if r(N) >= 2 {
            regress `var' year if areacode == `a'
            predict temp if areacode == `a'
            
            replace yhat = round(temp) if areacode == `a'
            drop temp
        }
        else {
            display "Skipping areacode `a' for `var' (not enough data)"
        }
    }

    replace `var'_reg = yhat if missing(`var')
    drop yhat
}


Nearest value

For the rest of the variables, if a observation is missing, I take the value of the previous year. For example, if some city had 2 libraries in 2015 and 2016 is N.A, then I suppose I also had 2 libraries in 2016.



* Nearest values
foreach var of varlist comm_centers libraries hospitals physicians dentists  {
    gen `var'_near = `var'
    bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_near = `var'_near[_n+1] if missing(`var'_near[1])
    bysort areacode (year): replace `var'_near = `var'_near[_n-1] if missing(`var'_near) 
}


Simple mean

I simply compute the mean for available years and fill it the remaining with it.



* Simple mean computation
foreach var of varlist fin_power {
    bysort areacode (year): egen `var'_mean = mean(`var')
}

Finally, I create new variables more suitable for analysis.

gen inmigration_rate = inmigrants_reg / pop_total_linear 
gen outmigration_rate = outmigrants_reg / pop_total_linear
gen net_migration_rate = inmigrants_reg / outmigrants_reg

gen dentists_pc = dentists_near / pop_total_linear
gen physicians_pc = physicians_near / pop_total_linear

Theoretical Part

Let \(p\) be the subjective probability of a earthquake to happen. \(p\) depends on \(i\), the information set available, and \(r\), the objective attributes that make such an event more likely. Then the HPF is:
\[P = P(Z, r, p(i, r))\]
Expected utility becomes:
\(EU = p(i, r) \cdot U^E[Z, r, Q] + (1 - p(i, r)) \cdot U^{NE}[Z, r, Q]\)
Given the budget constraint \(M = P(Z, r, p(i, r)) + Q\) (with \(Q\) being a composite commodity), the maximization of utility is given by:
\(\frac{\partial P}{\partial p}=\frac{U^{E} - U^{NE}}{p(i,r)\frac{\partial U^{E}}{\partial Q}+\left(1 - p(i,r)\right)\frac{\partial U^{NE}}{\partial Q}}\)

title: Design
text:
Let \(p\) denote the subjective probability that a property will be hit by an earthquake. \(p\) depends on both the information set \(i\) that individuals possess about seismic risks (such as recent events, media coverage, etc.), and \(r\), the objective attributes that make earthquakes more likely to occur, such as being located on seismic faults. Note that \(\pi\), the objective probability of an earthquake, is included in \(i\) in our case, since maps are provided and publicly available.
The Hedonic Price Function is given by: \[
P = P(Z, r, p(i, r))
\]
where \(P\) denotes the price of the house and \(Z\) denotes an additional set of structural, environmental, and locational characteristics not related to the seismic hazard maps.
Following Brookshire et al. (1985), the decision of agents is modeled using a state-dependent expected utility function: \[
EU = p(i, r) \cdot U^E[Z, r, Q] + (1 - p(i, r)) \cdot U^{NE}[Z, r, Q],
\] where \(U^E(\cdot)\) is the homeowner’s utility in the event of an earthquake, and \(U^{NE}(\cdot)\) is the homeowner’s utility when no such event occurs. Here, \(Q\) is a composite commodity, and the budget constraint of the homeowner is: \[
M = P(Z, r, p(i, r)) + Q.
\]
Maximizing expected utility with respect to \(p\), subject to the budget constraint, and then dividing by the expected marginal utility of income yields: \[
\frac{\partial P}{\partial p} = \frac{U^{E} - U^{NE}}{p(i,r)\frac{\partial U^{E}}{\partial Q} + \left(1 - p(i,r)\right)\frac{\partial U^{NE}}{\partial Q}}.
\]

title: Paper
text:
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Are people aware of the dangers they face? The theory states that a natural disaster acts as a signal, thus favoring the housing safety premium. Still, the extents of such a hypothesis remain to be seen. Does that information really affect people’s housing reponse?
While many studies have studied the direct
How housing markets react to natural disasters? While many studies have investigated the aftermaths of natural disasters, little is know about the potential differential price reaction gap between exposed and non exposed areas after such an event. Yet, in the short run, risk mapping might drive investment choices and therefore, in the long run, turn out to be one of the key factor of inequalities. With intensifying climate disasters, one may wonder how people are aware of climatic risks.
Japan is one the most prone-earthquake countries in the world.
In the short run, investors might want to avoid risky investments. In the long run, risk exposed areas might reconfigure spatial inequalities.
While most of the papers have studied the direct costs, some others investigate the indirect costs of
The paper proceeds as follows. Section II presents a brief theory of housing prices under uncertainty. Section III presents Japan’s background with earthquakes and describes the data. Section IV outlines my estimation strategy and identification assumptions. Section V presents the empirical results of the risk exposure effects on prices, and and Section VI presents results on time-on-the-market. Section VII uses robustness checks. Section VIII concludes.
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%% % SECTION II
Let denote \(p\) the subjective probability that a property will be hit by an earthquake. \(p\) depends on both the information set \(i\) individuals possess about the seismic risks (such as a recent event, media coverage, etc.), and \(r\) the objective attributes for earthquakes to be more likely to happen, such as being located right on seismic faults. Note that \(\pi\), the objective probability of an earthquake, is included in \(i\) in our case, since maps are provided and publicly available. Then the Hedonic Price Function is given by: [ P = P(Z, r, p(i, r)), ] with \(P\) denoting the price of the house and Z denoting an additional set of structural, environmental and locational characteristics not related to the seismic hazards maps. Following Brookshire et al. (1985), the decision of agents is modeled using a state dependent expected utlility function: [ EU = p(i, r) U^E[Z, r, Q] + (1 - p(i, r)) U^{NE}[Z, r, Q], ] where \(U^E(.)\) is the homeowner’s utility in a state in which a earthquake occurs and \(U^NE(.)\) is the homeowner’s utility when there is no such event. \(Q\) is a composite commodity, and the budget constraint of the homeowner is given by: [ M = P(Z, r, p(i, r)) + Q ] Maximising expected utility with respect to \(p\) subject to the budget constraint and then dividing through by the expected marginal utility of income results in the following expression: \[
\frac{\partial P}{\partial p}
=
\frac{U^{E} - U^{NE}}
{
p(i,r)\frac{\partial U^{E}}{\partial Q}
+
\left(1 - p(i,r)\right)\frac{\partial U^{NE}}{\partial Q}
}
\]
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%% % SECTION III
% Japan and earthquakes
% Data
I use data from , one the biggest real estate companies in Japan. The data consists in listings, covering all prefectures in Japan, from ?? 2015 to ?? 2023. The dataset contain ??? billions of observations, each includeind the house properties such as price and exact geolocation, among many other item (see Apendix for data details). While prices are only about estimation, the data also contains time the ad is available, which I will use as another outcome.
The risk maps are available from the . A webite was created in 2009 to inform people about seism risk, and everyone can take a look at the maps, easily downloadable. They have been updloaded every year since 2009 (expect with 2015). The maps have a high image resolution of 250 meters. There are many seismic risk measures. I choose to use the “probability that each site will be affected by an earthquake of seismic intensity 6 lower or more within 30 years,” as this one is described as representative by the J-SHIS itself (J-SHIS, insérer la réf ici).
To complete the above data, I use some other sources of data. First, I use data from which provides information about demographics, education, health and the labour market in Japan, aggregated at the city level. Data from the population census are available for 2015 and 2020. Other data are missing for some years. To deal with this issue, I use three ways. I use linear interpolation for the census missing values, linear regression and the nearest values for the other missing values. For example, number of habitants for 2023 is derived from the values of 2015 and 2020, number of migrants is derived using linear regression and the number of hospitals for a missing year takes the value of the previous year not missing. See Apendix for each case.
Macroeconomic variables are to take into account when it comes to the housing market. One of the most important is interest rate. Japan’s interest rate was extremely stable throughout the period covered by our data, around 1%. Additional macroeconomic variables include growth domestic product and “Indexes of Business Conditions” provided by e-stat. I choose the lagging index, see Apendix for data details.
% Sample selection I make a number of sample restrictions. I only keep observations that are about houses or appartments, discarding for example shop rents or parking sales. I decide to focus only on the private market, discarding public housing, and I also drop observations that consist in temporary accomodation such hotels or dormitories. You may find details about sample selection in the Apendix.
Risk maps are available each year. I first merge the Lifful Homes dataset with all the maps for every year. Then, I associate each observation to the right map according to the listing publication year.

\ln P_{it} = \alpha_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{J} \alpha_j Z_{ij} + \beta r_i + \gamma Exposed_i + \delta Post_{it} + \theta_t (Post_{it} \times Exposed_i) + \varepsilon_{it}
\end{equation}\]
\(P_{it}\) is the logarithm of the price of add \(i\) at time \(t\), \(Z\) is a set of structural characteristics of the good. \(Post_i\) takes 1 when the advertising is publised within 1 month after a big eartquake occurs.
